item.title: The Tripwire Patent
item.description:
Imagine a smoke alarm goes off in an office building at 2 a.m. Most of the time, it’s nothing — burnt toast in a break room, a dusty detector, a glitch. Dispatching a fire crew for every one of these wastes resources and dulls response to real emergencies. But ignoring it until someone calls again risks the opposite failure: the one night it isn’t nothing, help arrives late.

Emergency response systems have historically had only those two gears: respond now, or wait for a human to escalate. My patent — US 12,634,398 B2, granted May 19, 2026 and recognized with Motorola Solutions’ internal Patent of the Year award in 2022 — adds a third gear: watch, and act the moment the situation actually escalates.

The mechanism

When an emergency notification comes in — a 911 call, a triggered alarm — the system automatically retrieves sensor data associated with that exact time and place: nearby cameras, microphones, IoT sensors. But here’s the part that makes it a patent rather than a product brochure: it doesn’t use that data to immediately launch a response. It uses it to arm a trigger.

That trigger has two parts:

  1. An alarm-escalating condition, derived from the initial sensor data — what would “getting worse” look like for this incident, at this location?
  2. A matched response workflow — the specific incident-response process appropriate for that escalation type, pre-selected and ready to go.

Then the system waits. If second sensor data arrives that meets the armed condition — the smoke spreads to a second detector, the audio signature intensifies, movement patterns change — the workflow fires automatically, fully populated with the context first responders need.

The metaphor that stuck with me: it’s a high-tech tripwire. Not a camera that records what happened, not an alarm that cries wolf — a conditional watch, armed with local context, that converts to action if and only if the situation crosses the line it was armed to detect.

Why it matters

The naive version of “smart” emergency response — attach camera footage to the 911 call — gives dispatchers more to look at, but still leaves the escalation decision entirely on humans watching feeds. This design moves that judgment into the system for the window that matters most: the minutes after a first report when a minor incident either fizzles out or becomes the real thing. Dispatch capacity is finite; attention is finite. A system that knows what “worse” looks like — and stages the right response for it in advance — spends both only when they’re needed.

Credit where due: this was joint work with my co-inventors Emmy Beltre, Kaveh Malakuti, and Mariya Bondareva at Motorola Solutions. Filed September 2023, published April 2025, granted May 2026 — patents move at their own pace.

This is the first in what may become a series of plain-English explanations of my patents. The full list is on the about page.

item.title: Anatomy of Learning, and Downsides of Being a Second-Hand Thinker
item.description:
Growing up, I had a constant need of understanding how objects worked, I was constantly curious, was good at math in my peer group and dug my heels in to understand the core elements of any topic.

There though when I hit a wall for long time to learn a more complex topic, I had to make concessions on asking about the why, and accepting what was told as truth, that's just how it works and proceeding to use this assumed bedrock to then build the concepts atop.

This particularly happened to keep the pace with the fire hose of syllabus coming towards me, the need to keep with the pace of learning the course for the tests and an incomplete and immature system to digest the syllabus with a pin or a bookmark.

Now as I am older and am learning purely for the sake of understanding and out of curiosity for my selfish wants. I want to learn the concepts to their depth. I am not bound by a deadline on within which I have to learn, rather am bound by my limited time — limitations bring out efficiency. I hope to make use of my limited time well by making this an efficient use of time.

Downside of Second-Hand Thinking

Be a source, not a relay. — Grant Sanderson

By not understanding a topic completely and relying on another person’s explanation, I will not understand the topic at hand. I have not played with it enough to find the nook and cranny of the thought, the way the concept grows and expands, the beauty of it or where its weaknesses lie and where it would collapse. All these aspects are fun to understand, and give a different perspective to the topic at hand as well as a different view of the world.
